Authors: Xuyang Zheng; Ming Tang; Hao Chen; Ziyi Guo

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120220

Relevance: 0.627 (must_read)

Whether the abundant sulfate (S6+) of oxidized felsic magmas at convergent plate margins is sourced from subducted materials or generated through magmatic differentiation remains poorly understood. Here, we use S content in apatite to track the evolution of S redox state in felsic magmas over the past ∼120 Myr in the eastern Gangdese, southern Tibet. Unlike previous studies that rely on apatite grains separated from igneous rocks, we measure S in apatite inclusions hosted in detrital zircon. This approach enables us to assess magmatic S oxidation across the orogenic crust on a significantly broader scale than permitted by limited rock samples. Our results show two episodes of systematically elevated apatite S contents in the eastern Gangdese: one during the Late Cretaceous (95-80 Ma) and another from the Late Paleocene to the Miocene (45-10 Ma), following the India-Eurasia collision. This apatite S trend is consistent with that of the magma fO2 reconstructed using zircon Ce-U-Ti oxybarometer. More importantly, both apatite S content and magma fO2, on average, correlate with crustal thickness. While source composition and assimilation processes may contribute to redox heterogeneity in felsic magmas, our findings point to the critical role of crustal thickness in modulating the oxidation state of felsic magmas on an orogenic scale. Finally, by tracing oxidized S through detrital zircon samples, which are easy to collect and analytically efficient, our approach may allow for quick and effective evaluation of regional potential for porphyry copper mineralization.

item.title: Heavy sulfur isotope of arc ultramafic cumulates revealed by S-rich apatite from the Dongwanzi intrusion, North China
item.dc:creator: Huan Jennifer Chang
Mei-Fu Zhou
Wen-Jun Hu
Shengbiao Yang
Jian-Feng Gao
item.description: Arc magmas generally have higher sulfur (S) contents and higher δ34S values than mid-ocean ridge basalts. However, it remains debated whether the enrichment of S is due to crustal processes or mantle metasomatism. We present elemental and S-Sr isotopic compositions of apatite from the Early Permian Dongwanzi ultramafic intrusion, northern margin of the North China Craton. This arc intrusion is composed of clinopyroxenite and hornblendite based on textural relationships. Clinopyroxenites contains interstitial apatite and euhedral cumulus apatite. Interstitial apatite grains have high Cl (0.36–1.10 wt%), and Th (16.1–130 ppm), and La/Yb ratios (52.1–112), high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70370–0.70391), and low δ34SV-CDT values (−10.5 to +4.44 ‰), consistent with crystallization from late-stage trapped melts. In contrast, euhedral cumulus apatite grains have low Cl (0.23–0.71 wt%), high δ34SV-CDT values (∼ + 7.9 ‰), and La/Yb ratios (33.4–64.6), and low initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios (∼0.70337), indicative of growth from a relatively primitive magma. Compared to cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenites, cumulus apatite grains in hornblendites contain high F (1.45–2.69 wt%) and total REE + Y (1510–9830 ppm), and high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70333–0.70351), but slightly low Cl (0.01–0.50 wt%) and δ34SV-CDT (7.06 ‰ to 8.25 ‰), which were attributed to progressive fractional crystallization coupled with limited wall-rock contamination from clinopyroxenite to hornblendite. As a result, the consistently high δ34S values of cumulus apatite indicate that crustal contamination only caused a limited S isotope variation (∼1 ‰) during the formation of the Dongwanzi intrusion. Using S isotopic compositions of cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenite, the parental magma has δ34S values of ∼ + 7.9 ‰. Such high δ34S values are best explained by sub-arc mantle metasomatism rather than by crustal contamination. Collectively, our study highlights that S isotopic composition of apatite can be used to track the S isotope evolution and to constrain S sources in arc mafic-ultramafic cumulates. Published: 2026-07-13T15:31:57+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.624 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Huan Jennifer Chang; Mei-Fu Zhou; Wen-Jun Hu; Shengbiao Yang; Jian-Feng Gao

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123606

Relevance: 0.624 (must_read)

Arc magmas generally have higher sulfur (S) contents and higher δ34S values than mid-ocean ridge basalts. However, it remains debated whether the enrichment of S is due to crustal processes or mantle metasomatism. We present elemental and S-Sr isotopic compositions of apatite from the Early Permian Dongwanzi ultramafic intrusion, northern margin of the North China Craton. This arc intrusion is composed of clinopyroxenite and hornblendite based on textural relationships. Clinopyroxenites contains interstitial apatite and euhedral cumulus apatite. Interstitial apatite grains have high Cl (0.36–1.10 wt%), and Th (16.1–130 ppm), and La/Yb ratios (52.1–112), high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70370–0.70391), and low δ34SV-CDT values (−10.5 to +4.44 ‰), consistent with crystallization from late-stage trapped melts. In contrast, euhedral cumulus apatite grains have low Cl (0.23–0.71 wt%), high δ34SV-CDT values (∼ + 7.9 ‰), and La/Yb ratios (33.4–64.6), and low initial 87Sr/86Sr ratios (∼0.70337), indicative of growth from a relatively primitive magma. Compared to cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenites, cumulus apatite grains in hornblendites contain high F (1.45–2.69 wt%) and total REE + Y (1510–9830 ppm), and high 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.70333–0.70351), but slightly low Cl (0.01–0.50 wt%) and δ34SV-CDT (7.06 ‰ to 8.25 ‰), which were attributed to progressive fractional crystallization coupled with limited wall-rock contamination from clinopyroxenite to hornblendite. As a result, the consistently high δ34S values of cumulus apatite indicate that crustal contamination only caused a limited S isotope variation (∼1 ‰) during the formation of the Dongwanzi intrusion. Using S isotopic compositions of cumulus apatite in clinopyroxenite, the parental magma has δ34S values of ∼ + 7.9 ‰. Such high δ34S values are best explained by sub-arc mantle metasomatism rather than by crustal contamination. Collectively, our study highlights that S isotopic composition of apatite can be used to track the S isotope evolution and to constrain S sources in arc mafic-ultramafic cumulates.

item.title: Carboniferous to Permian Tectonic Transition of the North Tianshan Belt (NW China): Insights From an Inverted HT‐LP Metamorphic Sequence in the Harlik Range
item.dc:creator: Xinghua Ni
Bo Wang
Mathieu Soret
Dominique Cluzel
Yan Chen
Tingting Cao
Chengzao Jia
Dong Jia
Michel Faure
Jiashuo Liu
Yuxin Sun
Yingying Li
Mohamed Abu Anbar
item.description: ABSTRACT The North Tianshan Belt in the southwestern Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB) provides unique insights into crustal evolution and geodynamic processes of accretionary orogeny. Here, we present new structural, thermobarometric and geochronological data from the Xiaopu Metamorphic Complex (XMC) in the eastern North Tianshan. Structural analysis indicates that an initial shortening phase (D1), which produced NW‐SE‐striking folds, was followed by a sinistral transtension (D2). The latter is characterized by syn‐kinematic crystallization of peak mineral assemblages, suggesting an inverted Buchan‐type metamorphic sequence. Subsequent strike‐perpendicular extension (D3) likely facilitated the exhumation of the XMC. Phase modelling and geothermobarometry show a progressive increase in peak temperature from the andalusite–staurolite zone (~580°C) to the sillimanite–migmatite zone (~690°C) at nearly constant pressure (~4 kbar), indicating a high‐temperature and low‐pressure (HT‐LP) thermal regime consistent with an extensional setting. U–Pb dating on zircon, monazite, and apatite indicates that D2 occurred at ~330–310 Ma, coeval with the HT‐LP metamorphism (320–310 Ma), and that D3 occurred at ~285 Ma. Biotite 40 Ar/ 39 Ar dating confirms syn‐/post‐D3 cooling (298–275 Ma). We suggest that magmatic intrusion controlled by transtension at the mid‐crustal level and asymmetric exhumation likely formed the inverted Buchan‐type metamorphic sequence. Our new results, together with regional data, reveal the late Palaeozoic tectono‐metamorphic evolution of the southwestern CAOB, involving arc–arc accretion, back‐arc transtension, and post‐orogenic extension. These processes governed the tectonic transition from shortening to extension in the mid‐crust of arc systems and induced magmatic intrusion and crustal exhumation during the final stages of accretionary orogeny. Published: 2026-07-16T02:07:11+00:00 Venue: Journal of Metamorphic Geology Score: 0.618 (must_read)

Authors: Xinghua Ni; Bo Wang; Mathieu Soret; Dominique Cluzel; Yan Chen; Tingting Cao; Chengzao Jia; Dong Jia; Michel Faure; Jiashuo Liu; Yuxin Sun; Yingying Li; Mohamed Abu Anbar

Journal: Journal of Metamorphic Geology

DOI: 10.1111/jmg.70047

Relevance: 0.618 (must_read)

ABSTRACT The North Tianshan Belt in the southwestern Central Asian Orogenic Belt (CAOB) provides unique insights into crustal evolution and geodynamic processes of accretionary orogeny. Here, we present new structural, thermobarometric and geochronological data from the Xiaopu Metamorphic Complex (XMC) in the eastern North Tianshan. Structural analysis indicates that an initial shortening phase (D1), which produced NW‐SE‐striking folds, was followed by a sinistral transtension (D2). The latter is characterized by syn‐kinematic crystallization of peak mineral assemblages, suggesting an inverted Buchan‐type metamorphic sequence. Subsequent strike‐perpendicular extension (D3) likely facilitated the exhumation of the XMC. Phase modelling and geothermobarometry show a progressive increase in peak temperature from the andalusite–staurolite zone (~580°C) to the sillimanite–migmatite zone (~690°C) at nearly constant pressure (~4 kbar), indicating a high‐temperature and low‐pressure (HT‐LP) thermal regime consistent with an extensional setting. U–Pb dating on zircon, monazite, and apatite indicates that D2 occurred at ~330–310 Ma, coeval with the HT‐LP metamorphism (320–310 Ma), and that D3 occurred at ~285 Ma. Biotite 40 Ar/ 39 Ar dating confirms syn‐/post‐D3 cooling (298–275 Ma). We suggest that magmatic intrusion controlled by transtension at the mid‐crustal level and asymmetric exhumation likely formed the inverted Buchan‐type metamorphic sequence. Our new results, together with regional data, reveal the late Palaeozoic tectono‐metamorphic evolution of the southwestern CAOB, involving arc–arc accretion, back‐arc transtension, and post‐orogenic extension. These processes governed the tectonic transition from shortening to extension in the mid‐crust of arc systems and induced magmatic intrusion and crustal exhumation during the final stages of accretionary orogeny.

item.title: Genesis and geodynamic implications of Early Cretaceous granitoids in the southern Great Xing’an Range, NE China: The Laodaogou granitic complex
item.dc:creator: Guoxiong Ma
Qigui Mao
Tao Zou
Hadi Shafaii Moghadam
Yulong Jiang
Huailong Yang
Dunfeng Xiang
Meng Zhang
He Yang
Wenjiao Xiao
item.description: Mesozoic granitoids associated with Sn-Pb-Zn-Ag polymetallic deposits are prominent features in the southern Great Xing’an Range (SGXR), NE China. Understanding the origin and evolution of these granitoids is crucial for deciphering the associated mineralization and geodynamic processes. This study presents geological observations, zircon U-Pb ages, whole-rock major- and trace-element data, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopic analyses of the Early Cretaceous Laodaogou granitic complex in the SGXR. This complex, which hosts the Laodaogou Ag-Pb-Zn-(Sn) occurrence, consists of various types of plutonic rocks, including biotite monzogranites, biotite alkali-feldspar granites, alkali-feldspar granites, and porphyritic alkali-feldspar granitic dikes. Zircon U-Pb dating of these units yields Early Cretaceous ages ranging from ca. 145.3 Ma to 130.6 Ma. The four granite units exhibit broad variations in (K2O + Na2O)/CaO (5.63−56.64), FeOt/MgO (6.85−116.97), 10,000*Ga/Al (2.25−3.09), and Zr + Ce + Nb + Y contents (97.1−274.7 ppm). The elemental results coordinated U-Pb ages indicate that the Laodaogou complex exhibits high degrees of fractionation, evolving from I-type to A-type granites. The granite units show similar εHf(t) values (+7.7 to +16.1) and relatively young Hf model ages (650−165 Ma). It suggests that they were primarily derived from partial melting of a juvenile lower crust originating from a depleted mantle source. In the context of relevant published data, we propose that the retreating subduction of the Paleo-Pacific plate drove the Early Cretaceous magmatism in the SGXR. Asthenospheric mantle upwelling induced partial melting of the juvenile crust within the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, accompanied by magmatic differentiation and shallow emplacement. These processes culminated in regional Sn-Ag-Pb-Zn mineralization within the SGXR. Published: 2026-07-13T18:53:33+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.614 (must_read)

Authors: Guoxiong Ma; Qigui Mao; Tao Zou; Hadi Shafaii Moghadam; Yulong Jiang; Huailong Yang; Dunfeng Xiang; Meng Zhang; He Yang; Wenjiao Xiao

Journal: Geological Society of America Bulletin

DOI: 10.1130/b38322.1

Relevance: 0.614 (must_read)

Mesozoic granitoids associated with Sn-Pb-Zn-Ag polymetallic deposits are prominent features in the southern Great Xing’an Range (SGXR), NE China. Understanding the origin and evolution of these granitoids is crucial for deciphering the associated mineralization and geodynamic processes. This study presents geological observations, zircon U-Pb ages, whole-rock major- and trace-element data, and zircon Lu-Hf isotopic analyses of the Early Cretaceous Laodaogou granitic complex in the SGXR. This complex, which hosts the Laodaogou Ag-Pb-Zn-(Sn) occurrence, consists of various types of plutonic rocks, including biotite monzogranites, biotite alkali-feldspar granites, alkali-feldspar granites, and porphyritic alkali-feldspar granitic dikes. Zircon U-Pb dating of these units yields Early Cretaceous ages ranging from ca. 145.3 Ma to 130.6 Ma. The four granite units exhibit broad variations in (K2O + Na2O)/CaO (5.63−56.64), FeOt/MgO (6.85−116.97), 10,000*Ga/Al (2.25−3.09), and Zr + Ce + Nb + Y contents (97.1−274.7 ppm). The elemental results coordinated U-Pb ages indicate that the Laodaogou complex exhibits high degrees of fractionation, evolving from I-type to A-type granites. The granite units show similar εHf(t) values (+7.7 to +16.1) and relatively young Hf model ages (650−165 Ma). It suggests that they were primarily derived from partial melting of a juvenile lower crust originating from a depleted mantle source. In the context of relevant published data, we propose that the retreating subduction of the Paleo-Pacific plate drove the Early Cretaceous magmatism in the SGXR. Asthenospheric mantle upwelling induced partial melting of the juvenile crust within the Central Asian Orogenic Belt, accompanied by magmatic differentiation and shallow emplacement. These processes culminated in regional Sn-Ag-Pb-Zn mineralization within the SGXR.

item.title: Origin of the Caotan Fe-rich carbonatite in Qinling Orogenic Belt: Evidence from petrographic, mineralogical, and geochemical study
item.dc:creator: Danni Xu
Wenlei Song
Cheng Xu
Chunwan Wei
Shanna Xue
Le Qiao
Yajun Gao
item.description: Carbonatites are mantle-derived igneous rocks and important global resources of rare earth elements (REEs) and niobium (Nb). However, the processes responsible for the formation of iron (Fe)-rich carbonatites and their associated Fe mineralization remain poorly constrained. The Caotan carbonatites, located along the southern margin of the North China Block, are distinctly Fe-rich and lithologically diverse, comprising both calcite- and dolomite-dominated varieties. These features contrast sharply with nearby calcite-dominated carbonatites associated with Mo–REE–U–Pb polymetallic mineralization, such as those at Huanglongpu and Huayangchuan. This discrepancy suggests that the Caotan rocks followed a distinct magmatic evolutionary pathway, although their petrogenesis and the mechanisms responsible for Fe concentration remain unclear. In this study, detailed petrographic observations define an evolutionary sequence: dolomite–calcite carbonatite → dolomite carbonatite → magnetite–forsterite–calcite carbonatite → baryte- and tremolite-bearing calcite assemblages. Magnetite, the dominant Fe-bearing mineral, occurs as sparse, fine-grained crystals in the early dolomite-bearing carbonatites but becomes abundant in the magnetite–forsterite–calcite stage, where it forms euhedral to subhedral coarse phenocrysts containing ilmenite exsolution lamellae. Major-element and REE systematics of carbonates indicate intense fractional crystallization. Early crystallization and removal of Fe-poor calcite, accounting for up to approximately 75 vol% of the cumulate assemblage, progressively enriched the residual carbonatitic melt in Fe. Notably, in situ Sr isotopic analyses reveal a pronounced shift from less radiogenic initial values in the early carbonatites [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70411–0.70457] to significantly more radiogenic ratios in later-stage magnetite–forsterite–calcite, baryte–calcite, and tremolite–calcite assemblages [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70573–0.70604], indicating substantial crustal assimilation. We therefore propose that Fe enrichment was initially promoted by fractional crystallization of Fe-poor calcite, which progressively concentrated Fe in the residual melt. However, the critical trigger for magnetite saturation and Fe mineralization was subsequent interaction between the carbonatitic melt and surrounding wall rocks, leading to antiskarn formation, elevated silica activity, and ultimately magnetite precipitation. Published: 2026-07-15T15:35:55+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.601 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Danni Xu; Wenlei Song; Cheng Xu; Chunwan Wei; Shanna Xue; Le Qiao; Yajun Gao

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108712

Relevance: 0.601 (consider)

Carbonatites are mantle-derived igneous rocks and important global resources of rare earth elements (REEs) and niobium (Nb). However, the processes responsible for the formation of iron (Fe)-rich carbonatites and their associated Fe mineralization remain poorly constrained. The Caotan carbonatites, located along the southern margin of the North China Block, are distinctly Fe-rich and lithologically diverse, comprising both calcite- and dolomite-dominated varieties. These features contrast sharply with nearby calcite-dominated carbonatites associated with Mo–REE–U–Pb polymetallic mineralization, such as those at Huanglongpu and Huayangchuan. This discrepancy suggests that the Caotan rocks followed a distinct magmatic evolutionary pathway, although their petrogenesis and the mechanisms responsible for Fe concentration remain unclear. In this study, detailed petrographic observations define an evolutionary sequence: dolomite–calcite carbonatite → dolomite carbonatite → magnetite–forsterite–calcite carbonatite → baryte- and tremolite-bearing calcite assemblages. Magnetite, the dominant Fe-bearing mineral, occurs as sparse, fine-grained crystals in the early dolomite-bearing carbonatites but becomes abundant in the magnetite–forsterite–calcite stage, where it forms euhedral to subhedral coarse phenocrysts containing ilmenite exsolution lamellae. Major-element and REE systematics of carbonates indicate intense fractional crystallization. Early crystallization and removal of Fe-poor calcite, accounting for up to approximately 75 vol% of the cumulate assemblage, progressively enriched the residual carbonatitic melt in Fe. Notably, in situ Sr isotopic analyses reveal a pronounced shift from less radiogenic initial values in the early carbonatites [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70411–0.70457] to significantly more radiogenic ratios in later-stage magnetite–forsterite–calcite, baryte–calcite, and tremolite–calcite assemblages [(87Sr/86Sr)i = 0.70573–0.70604], indicating substantial crustal assimilation. We therefore propose that Fe enrichment was initially promoted by fractional crystallization of Fe-poor calcite, which progressively concentrated Fe in the residual melt. However, the critical trigger for magnetite saturation and Fe mineralization was subsequent interaction between the carbonatitic melt and surrounding wall rocks, leading to antiskarn formation, elevated silica activity, and ultimately magnetite precipitation.

item.title: Multi-component crustal recycling recorded by LiMg isotopes in Emeishan mafic–ultramafic layered intrusions with implications for Fe–Ti–V metallogeny
item.dc:creator: Yan-Fang Luo
Dong-Bo Tan
Hai-Ou Gu
Yang-Yang Wang
He Sun
Dong-Yong Li
Qian-Ling Zhou
Shi-Chu Zhao
Yilin Xiao
item.description: The Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP), generated by Late Permian mantle plume activity, hosts world-class Fe–Ti–V deposits within mafic–ultramafic layered intrusions. Although increasing evidence suggests that crustal materials play an important role in ore formation, the nature and proportion of crustal components incorporated into the mantle sources of these intrusions remain poorly constrained. Here we present whole-rock major and trace element data together with Li, Mg, Sr, and Nd isotopic compositions for four representative ELIP intrusions (Panzhihua, Baima, Taihe, and Hongge) to address this issue. These intrusions exhibit trace-element patterns and SrNd isotopic characteristics comparable to those of ELIP high-Ti basalts, indicating derivation from a broadly common mantle source. The samples display large variations in Li isotopic compositions (δ7Li = −6.5‰ to 6.6‰), whereas Mg isotopic compositions (δ26Mg = −0.42‰ to −0.09‰) remain within the range of depleted mantle values. After excluding the effects of low-temperature processes, including surface weathering and shallow crustal contamination, we propose that the mantle sources of these intrusions were metasomatized by recycled crustal materials, including altered oceanic crust and sediments. Mass-balance modeling indicates contributions of approximately 0.15–8.25% altered oceanic crust, 1.06–5.48% terrigenous sediments, and 0.70–2.92% pelagic sediments to the mantle source. Recycling of these components likely introduced volatiles and metal elements into the plume source and increased its oxidation state, thereby creating favorable conditions for Fe–Ti–V mineralization in the ELIP. These results highlight the key role of crustal recycling in modifying mantle plume sources and enhancing the metallogenic potential of large igneous province magmatic systems. Published: 2026-07-15T15:35:57+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.598 (consider)

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108713

Relevance: 0.598 (consider)

The Emeishan Large Igneous Province (ELIP), generated by Late Permian mantle plume activity, hosts world-class Fe–Ti–V deposits within mafic–ultramafic layered intrusions. Although increasing evidence suggests that crustal materials play an important role in ore formation, the nature and proportion of crustal components incorporated into the mantle sources of these intrusions remain poorly constrained. Here we present whole-rock major and trace element data together with Li, Mg, Sr, and Nd isotopic compositions for four representative ELIP intrusions (Panzhihua, Baima, Taihe, and Hongge) to address this issue. These intrusions exhibit trace-element patterns and SrNd isotopic characteristics comparable to those of ELIP high-Ti basalts, indicating derivation from a broadly common mantle source. The samples display large variations in Li isotopic compositions (δ7Li = −6.5‰ to 6.6‰), whereas Mg isotopic compositions (δ26Mg = −0.42‰ to −0.09‰) remain within the range of depleted mantle values. After excluding the effects of low-temperature processes, including surface weathering and shallow crustal contamination, we propose that the mantle sources of these intrusions were metasomatized by recycled crustal materials, including altered oceanic crust and sediments. Mass-balance modeling indicates contributions of approximately 0.15–8.25% altered oceanic crust, 1.06–5.48% terrigenous sediments, and 0.70–2.92% pelagic sediments to the mantle source. Recycling of these components likely introduced volatiles and metal elements into the plume source and increased its oxidation state, thereby creating favorable conditions for Fe–Ti–V mineralization in the ELIP. These results highlight the key role of crustal recycling in modifying mantle plume sources and enhancing the metallogenic potential of large igneous province magmatic systems.

item.title: Selenium content and enrichment processes recorded in the Magushan Cu-Mo skarn deposit, Xuancheng ore district, China
item.dc:creator: Yue Li
Feng Yuan
Simon M. Jowitt
Taofa Zhou
Xunyu Hu
Xiaohui Li
item.description: Selenium is a semi-metal that is often considered critical and is typically produced as a byproduct of copper production from porphyry-skarn deposits. The Magushan deposit is a skarn Cu-Mo deposit within the Middle and Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt in China that contains significant amounts of Se mineralization. This study focuses on the distribution and deportment of Se within the deposit, the factors controlling the variations of Se in the sulfides present at Magushan, and the source of Se. The deposit records pre-ore, Cu-Mo, Pb-Zn, and post-ore stages of mineralization, with the majority of Se mineralization associated with the Cu-Mo stage. Except for a very small amount of naumannite (Ag2Se) formed during the Pb-Zn stage, the Se within the Magushan system was mainly incorporated into Cu-Mo stage sulfides by isomorphic substitution for S. The Se content of different sulfides in the system is also likely controlled by variations in distribution coefficients, with Se more likely to be incorporated into pyrite within the Magushan deposit than other sulfides. The sulfides formed during the Pb-Zn stage of mineralization have significantly lower Se concentrations (<50 ppm) than those formed during the Cu-Mo stage of mineralization (molybdenite: 311.9−715.5 ppm Se; chalcopyrite: 325.2−742.3 ppm; pyrite: 221.8−833.5 ppm). This indicates that the Se content of these sulfides is controlled by variations in temperature, oxygen fugacity, and the Se concentration of the hydrothermal fluids that formed the deposit. The Se/S ratios and δ34S values of sulfides from the Magushan deposit (1.1‰−5.4‰ with a mean of 2.5‰; N = 38) also provide evidence of the magmatic sourcing of S and Se. These data also suggest that the assimilation of upper crustal rocks by parental magmas associated with the Se-bearing deposit may be a crucial process for the generation of Se mineralization. In short, the Se mineralization within the Magushan skarn system is related to a proximal porphyritic granodiorite, with the Se within the deposit predominantly hosted by sulfides. The Se concentrations of these sulfides are controlled by variations in temperature, oxygen fugacity, distribution coefficient, and the Se concentration of the hydrothermal fluids that formed the deposit. These characteristics should be used to guide both the exploration for, and the assessment of, the Se potential of skarn systems elsewhere within the Middle and Lower Yangtze River metallogenic belt in China and globally. Published: 2026-07-16T18:50:51+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.595 (consider)

This indicates that the Se content of these sulfides is controlled by variations in temperature, oxygen fugacity, and the Se concentration of the hydrothermal fluids that formed the deposit. The Se/S ratios and δ34S values of sulfides from the Magushan deposit (1.1‰−5.4‰ with a mean of 2.5‰; N = 38) also provide evidence of the magmatic sourcing of S and Se. item.title: Crustal growth and reworking of the Southern West African craton: Implications for regional tectonics
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Thébaud
Quentin Masurel
Luis Para-Avila
Aurélien Eglinger
Julien Perret
Augustin Yao Koffi
Ousmane Wane
Helen McFarlane
Graham Begg
Guillaume Duclaux
Mark Jessell
Olivier Vanderhaeghe
Lenka Baratoux
Mahamadou Diallo
Alain Kouamelan
Tony Kemp
Noreen J. Evans
Armin Zeh
item.description: This contribution reviews the crustal growth of the West African Craton and its geodynamic evolution using whole rock geochemistry together with U-Pb geochronological and Lu-Hf isotopic records preserved in igneous zircon grains from felsic plutonic rocks. Our study shows that the crustal growth in the southern West African Craton during the late Siderian and most of the Rhyacian (~2350-2300 Ma and ~2300-2060 Ma, respectively) was largely driven by the opening and subsequent closure of a large oceanic basin that fragmented the Neoarchean continental lithosphere. This tectonic evolution involved two major orogenic cycles including the Eoeburnean (~2285-2120 Ma) and Eburnean (~2120-2095 Ma) orogenies, each marked by distinct pulses of both juvenile magmatism and associated crustal reworking. Geochemical, geochronological, and isotopic data acquired on Eoeburnean plutonic rocks are used to investigate the process leading to crustal growth and its evolution during the Rhyacian. In central and western Ghana, central Cote d’Ivoire, and Burkina Faso, melt derivation from sources where hornblende and/or garnet were stable is consistent with crustal thickening and the injection of mantle derived magmas. In contrast, eastern and southeastern portions of the sWAC record reworking of Archean crust, as indicated by negative ƐHf values, whereas the western Kénéma-Man Domain over in Liberia and Sierra Leone exhibit intact Archean continental crust. During the Eburnean orogeny, more extensive crustal melting occurred at both deep and shallow structural levels, with ƐHf values indicating both continued juvenile input and reworking of earlier crust, including ongoing reworking of the northern margin of the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. Late-orogenic bimodal magmatism (~2095-2040 Ma) in southern Guinea is interpreted as a response to asthenospheric upwelling, likely triggered by lithospheric delamination or slab break-off following arc-continent collision. These magmatic rocks exhibit mixed isotopic signatures, consistent with contributions from both mantle-derived and reworked crustal sources. We propose that the tectonic evolution of the southern West African Craton was controlled by at least two partly synchronous subduction zone segments. The first was active during the Eoeburnean orogenic cycle and was southeast-dipping beneath the Archean BurGhana block in the eastern part of the craton. The second one was active during the Eburnean orogenic cycle and east-dipping, likely extending northwards into the Reguibat Domain and extended in the southern West African Craton where Precambrian terrains accreted onto the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. The convergence and diachronous collision of intra-oceanic arcs with Archean lithospheric blocks (Kénéma-Man, BurGhana, Reguibat) marked the protracted closure of a ~3,500 km wide oceanic basin. Published: 2026-07-13T05:52:38+00:00 Venue: Earth-Science Reviews Score: 0.595 (consider)

Evans; Armin Zeh

Relevance: 0.595 (consider)

This contribution reviews the crustal growth of the West African Craton and its geodynamic evolution using whole rock geochemistry together with U-Pb geochronological and Lu-Hf isotopic records preserved in igneous zircon grains from felsic plutonic rocks. Our study shows that the crustal growth in the southern West African Craton during the late Siderian and most of the Rhyacian (~2350-2300 Ma and ~2300-2060 Ma, respectively) was largely driven by the opening and subsequent closure of a large oceanic basin that fragmented the Neoarchean continental lithosphere. This tectonic evolution involved two major orogenic cycles including the Eoeburnean (~2285-2120 Ma) and Eburnean (~2120-2095 Ma) orogenies, each marked by distinct pulses of both juvenile magmatism and associated crustal reworking. Geochemical, geochronological, and isotopic data acquired on Eoeburnean plutonic rocks are used to investigate the process leading to crustal growth and its evolution during the Rhyacian. In central and western Ghana, central Cote d’Ivoire, and Burkina Faso, melt derivation from sources where hornblende and/or garnet were stable is consistent with crustal thickening and the injection of mantle derived magmas. In contrast, eastern and southeastern portions of the sWAC record reworking of Archean crust, as indicated by negative ƐHf values, whereas the western Kénéma-Man Domain over in Liberia and Sierra Leone exhibit intact Archean continental crust. During the Eburnean orogeny, more extensive crustal melting occurred at both deep and shallow structural levels, with ƐHf values indicating both continued juvenile input and reworking of earlier crust, including ongoing reworking of the northern margin of the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. Late-orogenic bimodal magmatism (~2095-2040 Ma) in southern Guinea is interpreted as a response to asthenospheric upwelling, likely triggered by lithospheric delamination or slab break-off following arc-continent collision. These magmatic rocks exhibit mixed isotopic signatures, consistent with contributions from both mantle-derived and reworked crustal sources. We propose that the tectonic evolution of the southern West African Craton was controlled by at least two partly synchronous subduction zone segments. The first was active during the Eoeburnean orogenic cycle and was southeast-dipping beneath the Archean BurGhana block in the eastern part of the craton. The second one was active during the Eburnean orogenic cycle and east-dipping, likely extending northwards into the Reguibat Domain and extended in the southern West African Craton where Precambrian terrains accreted onto the Archean Kénéma-Man Domain. The convergence and diachronous collision of intra-oceanic arcs with Archean lithospheric blocks (Kénéma-Man, BurGhana, Reguibat) marked the protracted closure of a ~3,500 km wide oceanic basin.

item.title: Paleoproterozoic orogenic gold system in the Contendas-Mirante belt, São Francisco Craton, Brazil: Insights from lithogeochemistry, U–Pb detrital zircon geochronology, and geodynamic analysis
item.dc:creator: Ricardo Ramos Spreafico
Astrid Holzheid
Elson Paiva Oliveira
Carlos Marcello Dias Fernandes
Carlos Eduardo Ganade de Araujo
Fabiano Cruz do Livramento
Antônio Marcos Vitória de Moraes
item.description: Orogenic gold systems are important sources of Paleoproterozoic gold mineralization in cratonic settings worldwide and are commonly associated with convergent-margin tectonics and crustal-scale fluid flow. In the northern São Francisco Craton, eastern Brazil, several Rhyacian-Orosirian gold occurrences are known; however, the geodynamic context and structural framework controlling mineralization in the Contendas-Mirante volcanosedimentary belt remain poorly constrained. This study integrates geological and structural mapping, core sample analysis, whole-rock geochemistry, U–Pb detrital zircon geochronology, and aerogeophysical data to constrain the tectonic setting and mineralization processes of the Contendas-Mirante belt. Metabasalts of the Neoarchean lower unit display light rare earth element-enriched patterns, elevated Th and U contents, and pronounced negative Nb–Ta–Ti anomalies, consistent with a continental geochemical signature. Elevated Th/Yb, La/Yb, and Th/Nb ratios may have been partly modified by secondary processes and are therefore considered supportive, but not restricted to an arc-like continental geochemical signature, as they may also reflect crustal contamination during the evolution of basaltic magma. U–Pb detrital zircon ages from associated phyllites yield a maximum depositional age of 2626 ± 31 Ma, indicating provenance from proximal Neoarchean volcanic sources along a continental margin. Structural analyses reveal a dominant north–south-trending crustal-scale lineament formed during the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event, itself associated with the accretion of oceanic and continental arcs and older continental blocks in the northern São Francisco Craton. Subordinate northwest-southeast and northeast-southwest structures controlled the distribution of lithologies and fluid migration. Gold mineralization in the Contendas-Mirante belt is hosted by brecciated ferruginous metacherts of the lower unit and is spatially associated with quartz-sericite schists, reflecting fluid focusing within brittle-ductile deformation zones where iron-rich sedimentary rocks acted as effective chemical and rheological traps. The results support a tectonic evolution involving Neoarchean continental-margin volcanism, followed by the Rhyacian-Orosirian tectonothermal event and orogenic gold mineralization, most likely within a continental arc system. The Contendas-Mirante belt mineralization exhibits key temporal, structural, and geodynamic characteristics of Paleoproterozoic orogenic gold systems in the Congo, Amazonian, and West African cratons, contributing to broader reconstructions of crustal growth, metallogenesis, and gold exploration models in the São Francisco Craton and analogous cratonic provinces worldwide. Published: 2026-07-10T19:28:39+00:00 Venue: Precambrian Research Score: 0.595 (consider)

item.title: Differential exhumation of giant lode gold deposits in the Jiaodong Peninsula, eastern China
item.dc:creator: Hongtao Zhao
Yu Zhang
Lin Gong
Martin Danišík
Yongjun Shao
Yuanming Pan
Yanbo Xu
Teimoor Nazari-Dehkordi
Yuzhou Feng
item.description: The Jiaodong Peninsula is China’s premier gold province, containing >5500 t of gold reserves, and exhibits pronounced spatial variation in lode gold mineralization. This variation is especially evident along the Jiaojia fault zone, where the northern segment hosts >90% of the gold endowment, while the southern segment is only sparsely mineralized. To understand this pattern, we integrate new apatite (U-Th)/He (AHe; n = 48 single-grain ages from eight samples, 50.1 ± 9.6 Ma to 2.8 ± 0.7 Ma) and zircon (U-Th)/He (ZHe; n = 40 single-grain ages from eight samples, 133.8 ± 6.1 Ma to 106.8 ± 5.0 Ma) ages obtained from a vertical drill-core profile (Dayinjia ZK32-1; surface to −1492 m depth) in the southern Jiaojia fault zone with a comprehensive low-temperature thermochronologic database (70 zircon fission-track [ZFT], 164 apatite fission-track [AFT], 84 ZHe, and 52 AHe). Thermal modeling reveals five distinct exhumation phases (120−95 Ma, 95−60 Ma, 60−45 Ma, 45−15 Ma, and 15 Ma to present) occurring in the southern segment. Regional analysis reveals substantially less post-mineralization exhumation along the southern Jiaojia fault zone relative to the northern segment. This disparity is likely responsible for the lower gold endowment found in the south. Regionally, two major post-mineralization exhumation episodes (110−70 Ma and 50−30 Ma) in the Jiaodong Peninsula reflect tectonic transitions driven by evolving subduction dynamics. These episodes indicate elevated exhumation rates and greater cumulative denudation in the eastern peninsula. This likely limited ore preservation, contributing to the heterogeneous distribution of gold resources across the peninsula. Notably, the older thermochronologic ages at the low elevations of key gold districts, such as Sanshandao, Jiaojia, and Linglong, suggest limited post-mineralization exhumation, which has contributed to the preservation of these ore deposits. These findings underscore the critical relationship between exhumation processes and the spatial variation in gold mineralization and demonstrate that low-temperature thermochronology is an effective tool for guiding the exploration of deep-lying lode gold deposits. Published: 2026-07-10T20:14:07+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.595 (consider)

item.title: Iron redox and hydrogen generation during multistage serpentinization under low silica activity: insights from the Heimulin serpentinites, central China
item.dc:creator: Meijun Gong
Kai Wu
Xinru Zhu
Chang Zhang
Chao Zhang
Hong Zhang
Yang Hu
Juan Zhang
Honglin Yuan
Weidong Sun
item.description: The redistribution and oxidation of iron during serpentinization are critical for quantifying hydrogen production and redox fluxes in subduction zones. This study investigates serpentinites from the Heimulin area in the Qinling Orogen, central China, to constrain their evolution and identify the factors controlling Fe3+ incorporation into serpentine minerals and associated H2 generation. Whole-rock geochemical compositions and the chemistry of relict primitive Cr-spinel domains indicate that the protoliths were highly refractory forearc mantle peridotites. The enrichment patterns of fluid-mobile elements closely resemble those of forearc serpentinites such as those from the Mariana, suggesting the protolith was hydrated by slab-derived fluids in a shallow forearc region. Mineralogical analyses identify four distinct types of lizardite, reflecting the sequence of serpentinization. Initial low-temperature serpentinization produced some Type-1 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through dioctahedral substitution. When silica activity became sufficiently low, hydration of olivine produced Fe-rich brucite and some Type-1 and Type-2 lizardite grains that lack evident Fe3+ substitution. As serpentinization progressed, local increases in silica activity, driven by mineralogical heterogeneity (e.g., orthopyroxene dissolution) or influx of SiO2-rich external fluids, induced breakdown of Fe-rich brucite and promoted the formation of fine-grained magnetite and some Type-2 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through cronstedtite substitution. Since redox reactions primarily occur during interactions between brucite and SiO2-rich fluids, and given the widespread occurrence of cronstedtite-type substitution in natural lizardite, the formation of Fe3+-serpentine with tetrahedral Fe3+ may indicate that hydrogen production has reached the main stage. During subsequent recrystallization, the Fe3+-deficient environment favored the transformation of lizardite into chrysotile asbestos. Simultaneously, silica-poor conditions promoted the preservation of brucite, which aggregated and recrystallized to form economically significant fibrous brucite deposits. However, the chemical compositions of lizardite in recrystallized serpentinites, together with bulk-rock Fe3+/ΣFe ratios, indicate that Fe3+ was redistributed during recrystallization. Overall, low-temperature serpentinization of refractory peridotites under SiO2-poor conditions, followed by recrystallization, promotes the formation of economically valuable asbestos and brucite fibers. The redistribution and oxidation of iron during serpentinization are critical for quantifying hydrogen production and redox fluxes in subduction zones. This study investigates serpentinites from the Heimulin area in the Qinling Orogen, central China, to constrain their evolution and identify the factors controlling Fe3+ incorporation into serpentine minerals and associated H2 generation. Mineralogical analyses identify four distinct types of lizardite, reflecting the sequence of serpentinization. When silica activity became sufficiently low, hydration of olivine produced Fe-rich brucite and some Type-1 and Type-2 lizardite grains that lack evident Fe3+ substitution. As serpentinization progressed, local increases in silica activity, driven by mineralogical heterogeneity (e.g., orthopyroxene dissolution) or influx of SiO2-rich external fluids, induced breakdown of Fe-rich brucite and promoted the formation of fine-grained magnetite and some Type-2 lizardite grains, in which Fe3+ was incorporated into the crystal lattice through cronstedtite substitution. Since redox reactions primarily occur during interactions between brucite and SiO2-rich fluids, and given the widespread occurrence of cronstedtite-type substitution in natural lizardite, the formation of Fe3+-serpentine with tetrahedral Fe3+ may indicate that hydrogen production has reached the main stage. During subsequent recrystallization, the Fe3+-deficient environment favored the transformation of lizardite into chrysotile asbestos. However, the chemical compositions of lizardite in recrystallized serpentinites, together with bulk-rock Fe3+/ΣFe ratios, indicate that Fe3+ was redistributed during recrystallization. Overall, low-temperature serpentinization of refractory peridotites under SiO2-poor conditions, followed by recrystallization, promotes the formation of economically valuable asbestos and brucite fibers.

item.title: Geotectonic−metallogenic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys in the northern Tibetan Plateau: A synthesis from divergent to convergent settings
item.dc:creator: Wenyuan Li
Bing Qian
Matthew J. Brzozowski
Minxin You
Lu Li
item.description: The tectonic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys Ocean and its associated metallogenic processes in the northern Tibetan Plateau are pivotal topics within the field of Earth sciences. Despite recent advances, a detailed understanding of its evolutionary history and metallogenic mechanisms remains elusive. This contribution, grounded in the metallogenic characteristics of the northern Tibetan Plateau from the late Paleozoic to early Mesozoic, provides a systematic dissection of the tectonic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys and its profound impact on the metallogenesis of this region. The study identifies two branches of the Paleo-Tethys. The northern branch, situated between the Kangxiwa−Animaqing−Mianlue suture zone and the Xijingwulan−Jinshajiang−Ganzi−Litang suture zone, represents the main ocean basin, whereas the southern branch, situated within the Longmu Co−Shuanghu suture zone between the Northern and Southern Qiangtang terranes, formed through post-arc expansion. This contribution not only clarifies the timing of initiation (Early Devonian) and closure (Late Triassic) of the Paleo-Tethys but also categorizes its metallogenic processes into two major types based on tectonic cycles—those associated with divergent settings and those associated with convergent settings. Metallogenesis associated with divergent settings is exemplified by the super-large Xiarihamu magmatic Ni-Cu-Co sulfide deposit, underscoring the role of Paleo-Tethys rifting in this ocean basin’s metallogenic evolution. In contrast, metallogenesis associated with convergent settings is characterized by numerous medium- to small-scale late Permian to Triassic porphyry−skarn−polymetallic deposits and pegmatite-hosted Li-Be deposits, highlighting the significant contributions of oceanic subduction and collisional orogeny to mineralization. The discovery of Triassic super-large orogenic Au deposits in the Eastern Kunlun and Western Qinling orogens, as well as the pegmatite-hosted Li deposits at Dahongliutan and Jiajika, located in the Western Kunlun orogen and Songpan−Ganzi orogen, respectively, further supports the intimate link between the tectonic and metallogenic evolution of the Paleo-Tethys. Published: 2026-07-13T18:53:32+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.581 (consider)

item.title: Controls on REE enrichment in post-collisional alkaline rocks by subduction-related metasomatism: insights from the Zhuopan Complex, southeastern Tibetan plateau
item.dc:creator: Zheng Liu
Lin Sun
Wei Zhang
Qing-He Yan
Guo-Chang Wang
Hao Liu
Han Zhang
Yan Yang
item.description: Alkaline complexes host important economic rare earth element (REE) resources which are of critical strategic importance in modern technology and industry. The REE mineralization associated with alkaline rocks is generally formed in the post-collision settings, but the mechanisms of REE enrichment in their source region remain poorly constrained. The Paleogene Zhuopan alkaline complex hosting the Yongping REE deposit was emplaced in a post-collisional setting following the Indo-Asian continental collision. This complex exhibits geochemical characteristics enriched in K2O and incompatible elements (including large-ion lithophile elements, high-field-strength elements, and REEs), coupled with enriched Sr-Nd-Hf isotopic compositions and crust-like REE and trace element signatures. The Zhuopan alkaline complex originated from low-degree partial melts of an enriched lithospheric mantle source (garnet-phlogopite-amphibole lherzolite) modified by subduction slab-released fluids, which subsequently underwent fractional crystallization. The geochemically consistent REE enrichment in the Zhuopan complex was not primarily controlled by shallow-level magmatic processes, but rather via preferential extraction from amphibole-bearing metasomatic mantle during melting events. Globally distributed alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits demonstrate geochemical affinities comparable to those of the Zhuopan complex. These findings highlight that ancient subduction-related metasomatic processes exert critical control on the formation of post-collisional alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits worldwide. Published: 2026-07-14T15:55:20+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.580 (consider)

Alkaline complexes host important economic rare earth element (REE) resources which are of critical strategic importance in modern technology and industry. The REE mineralization associated with alkaline rocks is generally formed in the post-collision settings, but the mechanisms of REE enrichment in their source region remain poorly constrained. The Paleogene Zhuopan alkaline complex hosting the Yongping REE deposit was emplaced in a post-collisional setting following the Indo-Asian continental collision. This complex exhibits geochemical characteristics enriched in K2O and incompatible elements (including large-ion lithophile elements, high-field-strength elements, and REEs), coupled with enriched Sr-Nd-Hf isotopic compositions and crust-like REE and trace element signatures. The Zhuopan alkaline complex originated from low-degree partial melts of an enriched lithospheric mantle source (garnet-phlogopite-amphibole lherzolite) modified by subduction slab-released fluids, which subsequently underwent fractional crystallization. The geochemically consistent REE enrichment in the Zhuopan complex was not primarily controlled by shallow-level magmatic processes, but rather via preferential extraction from amphibole-bearing metasomatic mantle during melting events. Globally distributed alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits demonstrate geochemical affinities comparable to those of the Zhuopan complex. These findings highlight that ancient subduction-related metasomatic processes exert critical control on the formation of post-collisional alkaline rocks hosting REE deposits worldwide.

item.title: First-principle study of mineral-solution equilibrium isotope fractionations of magnesium, sulfate and calcium in CaCO3
item.dc:creator: Chirantan Pramanik
Itay Halevy
item.description: Chemical impurities in CaCO 3 minerals, present as ion substitutions (e.g., Mg 2 + -for-Ca 2 + , SO 4 2 − -for-CO 3 2 − ) are common. Impurities incorporated into a carbonate lattice have a different physicochemical environment than their aqueous forms, resulting in isotopic fractionations of these impurity atoms between the mineral and its parent fluid. Moreover, the lattice distortion in the presence of impurities is proportional to the impurity concentrations, potentially leading to a concentration-dependent effect on the mineral-fluid isotopic fractionation of the impurities and the major elements in the minerals. A mechanistic understanding of the effects of impurities in CaCO 3 minerals on the mineral lattice and the isotopic fractionation of the impurities and major elements is necessary for the use of carbonate minerals and their trace elements as paleo-environmental archives. The difficulty in achieving isotopic equilibrium during experimental precipitation of carbonate minerals motivates the calculation of equilibrium isotope effects by ab initio density functional theory (DFT) methods. However, the utility of DFT calculations in constraining the effects of impurity elements in carbonate minerals on the isotopic fractionation of both the impurities themselves and the major elements in the mineral is limited by computational feasibility. Even a single substitution in a model lattice, composed of as many atoms as computationally feasible for DFT calculations, results in impurity concentrations that are higher than those typical of most natural and experimental samples. Using DFT, we systematically modeled a range of calcite and aragonite supercell sizes with a single Mg or carbonate-associated sulfate (CAS) impurity, allowing calculation of the dependence of the CaCO 3 -fluid isotopic fractionation on the impurity concentration. We studied the change in the impurity bonding environment and computed phonon frequencies to calculate the reduced partition function ratios and the fractionation of S, Mg and Ca isotopes between the CaCO 3 and its parent fluid. We obtained a near-constant CAS-aqueous SO 4 2 − S isotope fractionation in calcite of 2.9‰ over a range of CAS concentrations between 26400 and 58600 ppm (sulfate mole fractions of 1/36 and 1/16), in close agreement with the highest-CAS experimental value of 2.9‰. We found a constant mineral-aqueous Mg 2 + Mg isotope fractionation over the range of modeled impurity concentrations of ∼ –8.6‰ in calcite and ∼ –7.2 to –8.8‰ in aragonite, in agreement with previous computational studies with similar Mg concentrations. We find a difference of up to ∼ 2.1‰ in 1000ln 44 α calcite and 1000ln 44 α aragonite between pure calcite and aragonite supercells and supercells of those minerals with 1/24 and 1/32 mole fractions of sulfate, respectively. A difference of up to ∼ 1‰ of 1000ln 44 α calcite and 1000ln 44 α aragonite from pure calcite and aragonite is observed with similar mole fractions of magnesium. These results highlight the effects of varying impurity concentrations in CaCO 3 minerals on the mineral-solution equilibrium isotopic fractionations of impurities and major elements, and the importance of understanding such effects for interpreting major and trace element isotopic variability in environmental and geological samples. Published: 2026-07-14T06:18:44+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.579 (consider)

However, the utility of DFT calculations in constraining the effects of impurity elements in carbonate minerals on the isotopic fractionation of both the impurities themselves and the major elements in the mineral is limited by computational feasibility. item.title: Time series analysis of mantle cycles, Part III: The 90 Myr cycle in the frequency of igneous zircon ages
item.dc:creator: Kent C. Condie
Stephen J. Puetz
item.description: A 90 Myr quasi-periodic cycle in zircon ages from igneous rocks has persisted for at least 3500 Myr. Although some age peaks from the 90 Myr cycle may correspond to the supercontinent cycle, there is no obvious relationship with the assembly or breakup phases. Our time-series of the globality of igneous activity shows that even the largest igneous events comprise less than 36% of the continental crust by volume. This implies that igneous events are regional rather than global in extent. The globality estimates of large igneous provinces are also rather small, in the range of 5%–15%, having a far smaller sample size than that from the igneous database. Another time-series shows that peaks in surface area of orogenic activity fluctuate proportionally to variation in igneous activity. This correlation indicates that the tectonic processes that trigger orogenic activity are closely associated with global variation in igneous activity. A dominant mantle event at 2700 Ma resulted in a rapid increase in the orogen/terrane area ratio, perhaps reflecting initiation of widespread plate tectonics. Considering the collective results from all time-series, the tectonic activity associated with the 90 Myr cycle appears to be regional and in any case not global. Both collisional and accretionary phases of orogens are characterized by mixed ε Hf(t) and ε Nd(t) values, positive in some instances and negative in others, with no discernable pattern. The large troughs in ε Nd(t) values at 2000–1800 Ma and 600–350 Ma correlate with the assembly of supercontinents Columbia and Gondwana-Pangea, respectively, and peaks in ε Nd(t) with positive signatures (1400, 1200, 800, and 700 Ma) correspond to the breakup of these two supercontinents. Although some of the peaks from the 90 Myr model have positive ε Hf(t) and ε Nd(t) and negative δ 18O suggesting juvenile crustal additions, they do not require net growth of juvenile crust because other peaks that align with this cycle have crustal reworking signatures. We speculate that the 90 Myr cycle is linked to periodic mantle magma generation caused by thermal instabilities in the deep mantle. This process could be reinforced by a related cycle in mantle plumes. It is also possible that some episodes of subduction, slab avalanches, and other upstairs-driven mantle events are at least partly controlled by an already existing 90 Myr cycle produced by these thermal instabilities in the deep mantle. Published: 2026-07-13T06:34:47+00:00 Venue: Geoscience Frontiers Score: 0.577 (consider)

item.title: Geochemical constraints on the Yangtze–Cathaysia block boundary from early paleozoic I-type granitoids across the Chenzhou–Linwu fault
item.dc:creator: Yi-Xin Liu
Xiu-Quan Miao
Yang Yu
Xiao-Long Huang
item.description: Pre-existing sutures, as the fundamental lithospheric boundaries, potentially represent lithospheric weakened zones that are easily to be reactivated and transformed, playing a crucial role in the evolution of continental blocks. In the South China Block (SCB), the Neoproterozoic suture between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks is well defined in the northeast along the Jiangshan–Shaoxing–Pingxiang fault (JSPF). However, whether this suture extends southward to the Chenzhou–Linwu fault (CLF) remains controversial, limiting our understanding of the tectonic evolution of the SCB. Thus, we present zircon UPb ages and HfO isotopic data, together with whole-rock geochemistry and SrNd isotopic data, for the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids located east of the CLF and the Daning granitoids situated to the west, respectively. Zircon UPb ages indicate that the studied granitoids were emplaced at ca. 443–435 Ma. All granitoids are I-type granitoids derived from the partial melting of ancient igneous basement. The high Y + Nb contents (144–593 ppm) of these granitoids, a planar distribution of Early Paleozoic granites in the SCB, and the absence of subduction-related records collectively support an intracontinental orogen for the Wuyi-Yunkai orogen (WYO). Furthermore, the Shangbao and Guidong granitoids, together with western Cathaysia I-type granitoids, exhibit lower Pb, Th, and U concentrations, and more negative εNd(t) and εHf(t) values than the Daning granitoids and eastern Yangtze I-type granitoids. This highlights a compositional contrast in crustal basements between the eastern and western segments of the CLF, characterized by minimal juvenile input in the former and a juvenile materials-modified basement in the latter. These findings suggest that the boundary between the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks extends southward to the CLF, providing insights into the orogenic processes of the WYO, and the role of weak lithospheric boundaries in controlling asthenospheric upwelling and emplacement of mantle-derived magmas. Published: 2026-07-16T05:29:54+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.575 (consider)

item.title: Sequential serpentinization reactions revealed by boron isotope systematics of the Lost City vent fluids
item.dc:creator: Aled D. Evans
Heather J. Goring-Harford
Rosalind M. Coggon
Rachael H. James
Caitlin McCarthy
Matthew J. Cooper
Gavin L. Foster
Gretchen L. Früh-Green
Susan Q. Lang
Damon A.H. Teagle
item.description: Hydrothermal vent fluids reflect interactions between oceanic lithosphere and seawater, but to date, the processes resulting in unusual hyperalkaline (up to pH 11) fluids venting ultramafic-hosted systems, such as the Lost City Hydrothermal Field, remain enigmatic. To elucidate fluid reaction pathways, we present boron (B) concentration and isotope analyses from fluids venting the Lost City, located off axis atop the serpentinized Atlantis Massif just west of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge at 30°N. Our data reveal that fluid B concentrations are markedly lower and isotopically lighter than that of seawater. These data contrast with the accepted understanding of B isotope systematics that predicts that the progressive removal of B from seawater during serpentine formation should result in low-B fluids with δ11B values higher than that of seawater. We utilize a mechanistic B isotope fractionation model coupled with a Bayesian inversion approach to robustly evaluate fluid-rock interactions and propose that Lost City fluid B compositions reflect a two-step process. Initially, B is progressively removed from seawater during serpentine formation, resulting in the generation of an effectively B-free serpentinizing fluid. Subsequently, B remobilized from previously formed serpentine is transferred to later fluids, resulting in the low-δ11B end-member values analyzed. Such an evolution demonstrates that fluid-rock interactions both add fluid-mobile elements such as B to mantle peridotites as well as remobilize them. Thus, analyzed serpentine δ11B signatures reflect the cumulative impact of B-consuming serpentinization and later B-remobilizing serpentinite recrystallization. Consequently, serpentinite alteration, in addition to serpentinization hydration reactions, actively contributes to chemical exchange between the mantle and the oceans. Published: 2026-07-10T21:19:01+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Aled D. Evans; Heather J. Goring-Harford; Rosalind M. Coggon; Rachael H. James; Caitlin McCarthy; Matthew J. Cooper; Gavin L. Foster; Gretchen L. Früh-Green; Susan Q. Lang; Damon A.H. Teagle

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54880.1

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Hydrothermal vent fluids reflect interactions between oceanic lithosphere and seawater, but to date, the processes resulting in unusual hyperalkaline (up to pH 11) fluids venting ultramafic-hosted systems, such as the Lost City Hydrothermal Field, remain enigmatic. To elucidate fluid reaction pathways, we present boron (B) concentration and isotope analyses from fluids venting the Lost City, located off axis atop the serpentinized Atlantis Massif just west of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge at 30°N. Our data reveal that fluid B concentrations are markedly lower and isotopically lighter than that of seawater. These data contrast with the accepted understanding of B isotope systematics that predicts that the progressive removal of B from seawater during serpentine formation should result in low-B fluids with δ11B values higher than that of seawater. We utilize a mechanistic B isotope fractionation model coupled with a Bayesian inversion approach to robustly evaluate fluid-rock interactions and propose that Lost City fluid B compositions reflect a two-step process. Initially, B is progressively removed from seawater during serpentine formation, resulting in the generation of an effectively B-free serpentinizing fluid. Subsequently, B remobilized from previously formed serpentine is transferred to later fluids, resulting in the low-δ11B end-member values analyzed. Such an evolution demonstrates that fluid-rock interactions both add fluid-mobile elements such as B to mantle peridotites as well as remobilize them. Thus, analyzed serpentine δ11B signatures reflect the cumulative impact of B-consuming serpentinization and later B-remobilizing serpentinite recrystallization. Consequently, serpentinite alteration, in addition to serpentinization hydration reactions, actively contributes to chemical exchange between the mantle and the oceans.

item.title: Fe–Mg isotope constraints on mantle processes and tectonic evolution of the Yilashan ophiolite, Tibet, China
item.dc:creator: Xiangzhen Xu
Fahui Xiong
Basem Zoheir
Tian Qiu
Huidan Xie
Weibin Gui
Jingsui Yang
item.description: The mantle processes and tectonic evolution of ophiolites in the Bangong–Nujiang suture zone remain debated, particularly regarding melt extraction, metasomatism, and chromitite formation in supra-subduction zone (SSZ) settings. Conventional tracers often fail to distinguish overprinting processes. Fe–Mg stable isotopes, which are sensitive to both partial melting and fluid/melt–rock interaction, offer a novel tool to fingerprint these processes. Here we present integrated Fe–Mg isotopic data, whole-rock geochemistry, and platinum-group element (PGE) systematics for harzburgites and chromitites from the Yilashan ophiolite (central Bangong–Nujiang suture zone) to address these issues. Harzburgites display near-chondritic δ56Fe values (0.02–0.09‰) and limited δ26Mg variability (–0.06 to 0.10‰), consistent with residues after high-degree melt extraction. Their depleted major element compositions, together with enrichment in large-ion lithophile elements and depletion in high-field-strength elements, require subsequent subduction-related metasomatism. In contrast, chromitites exhibit systematically lighter δ56Fe values (down to –0.31‰) and a wide range of δ26Mg values (–0.25 to 0.29‰), indicating pronounced isotopic heterogeneity. This variability is best explained by melt–rock interaction within focused melt pathways, consistent with the spatial association of chromitites with dunite channels, whereas alteration effects are not dominant but cannot be fully excluded. PGE systematics, including IPGE enrichment and distinct Pd/Ir–Pt/Pt* relationships, support chromitite formation from primitive boninitic melts, while harzburgite PGE patterns indicate melt infiltration or refertilization. Together, these data define a multi-stage evolutionary framework involving mantle depletion, subduction-related metasomatism, chromitite formation, and subsequent modification during cooling. Given current data limitations, this framework should be regarded as a working hypothesis requiring further testing. Published: 2026-07-15T15:41:57+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.574 (consider)

Authors: Xiangzhen Xu; Fahui Xiong; Basem Zoheir; Tian Qiu; Huidan Xie; Weibin Gui; Jingsui Yang

Journal: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences

DOI: 10.1016/j.jseaes.2026.107220

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

The mantle processes and tectonic evolution of ophiolites in the Bangong–Nujiang suture zone remain debated, particularly regarding melt extraction, metasomatism, and chromitite formation in supra-subduction zone (SSZ) settings. Conventional tracers often fail to distinguish overprinting processes. Fe–Mg stable isotopes, which are sensitive to both partial melting and fluid/melt–rock interaction, offer a novel tool to fingerprint these processes. Here we present integrated Fe–Mg isotopic data, whole-rock geochemistry, and platinum-group element (PGE) systematics for harzburgites and chromitites from the Yilashan ophiolite (central Bangong–Nujiang suture zone) to address these issues. Harzburgites display near-chondritic δ56Fe values (0.02–0.09‰) and limited δ26Mg variability (–0.06 to 0.10‰), consistent with residues after high-degree melt extraction. Their depleted major element compositions, together with enrichment in large-ion lithophile elements and depletion in high-field-strength elements, require subsequent subduction-related metasomatism. In contrast, chromitites exhibit systematically lighter δ56Fe values (down to –0.31‰) and a wide range of δ26Mg values (–0.25 to 0.29‰), indicating pronounced isotopic heterogeneity. This variability is best explained by melt–rock interaction within focused melt pathways, consistent with the spatial association of chromitites with dunite channels, whereas alteration effects are not dominant but cannot be fully excluded. PGE systematics, including IPGE enrichment and distinct Pd/Ir–Pt/Pt* relationships, support chromitite formation from primitive boninitic melts, while harzburgite PGE patterns indicate melt infiltration or refertilization. Together, these data define a multi-stage evolutionary framework involving mantle depletion, subduction-related metasomatism, chromitite formation, and subsequent modification during cooling. Given current data limitations, this framework should be regarded as a working hypothesis requiring further testing.

item.title: Neoproterozoic lamprophyre from the Southern Granulite Terrane, India: Implications for calc-alkaline magmatism in an aborted rift setting
item.dc:creator: M.R. Nandu
Y. Anilkumar
M.P. Manu Prasanth
Cheng-Xue Yang
M. Santosh
Sanghoon Kwon
Sung Won Kim
A.P. Pradeepkumar
Yoshiyuki Iizuka
S. Anbazhagan
Tamilarasan Kuppusamy
item.description: Calc-alkaline lamprophyres, owing to their distinctive geochemical signature, serve as crucial probes for deciphering continental-scale geodynamic processes as well as the tectonomagmatic evolution of the continental lithosphere. Here we present petrological, mineral chemical, whole-rock geochemical, and U-Pb zircon geochronological data on the calc-alkaline lamprophyre (spessartite) from the Salem Mafic–Ultramafic Complex (SMUC) of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT), India. The Salem lamprophyre exhibits a characteristic porphyritic to panidiomorphic texture dominated by amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) phenocrysts. Clinopyroxene (diopside) occurs as microphenocrysts, while feldspar (albite) is largely confined to the groundmass. The high Mg# values (80–83) and elevated concentrations of compatible trace elements (Cr and Ni) indicate the primitive nature of the melt, whereas the incompatible trace element ratios exclude any significant crustal contamination and closely resemble those of subduction-related calc-alkaline lamprophyres reported globally. Elevated Ba/Nb and low Nb/La ratios indicate derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source. The pronounced negative Ti–Nb–Ta and Zr-Hf anomalies, together with diagnostic geochemical ratios (e.g., Hf/Sm, Ta/La, Nb/Yb, La/Nb, Ba/Nb), suggest that the mantle source was metasomatized predominantly by dehydrated fluids released from a subducting slab. Zircon grains from the Salem lamprophyre exhibit well-developed magmatic crystallization features, and LA–ICP–MS U-Pb analyses yield a weighted mean age of 805 ± 2.7 Ma (MSWD = 0.54), which is comparable with the Tonian (ca. 830–720 Ma) alkaline–carbonatite magmatism in the region. We envisage that the Neoarchean subduction-modified lithospheric mantle was reactivated and partially melted during Neoproterozoic extension to generate the hydrous parental magma of the Salem calc-alkaline lamprophyre. Published: 2026-07-10T15:36:16+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.569 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M.R. Nandu; Y. Anilkumar; M.P. Manu Prasanth; Cheng-Xue Yang; M. Santosh; Sanghoon Kwon; Sung Won Kim; A.P. Pradeepkumar; Yoshiyuki Iizuka; S. Anbazhagan; Tamilarasan Kuppusamy

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108710

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

Calc-alkaline lamprophyres, owing to their distinctive geochemical signature, serve as crucial probes for deciphering continental-scale geodynamic processes as well as the tectonomagmatic evolution of the continental lithosphere. Here we present petrological, mineral chemical, whole-rock geochemical, and U-Pb zircon geochronological data on the calc-alkaline lamprophyre (spessartite) from the Salem Mafic–Ultramafic Complex (SMUC) of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT), India. The Salem lamprophyre exhibits a characteristic porphyritic to panidiomorphic texture dominated by amphibole (magnesio-hornblende) phenocrysts. Clinopyroxene (diopside) occurs as microphenocrysts, while feldspar (albite) is largely confined to the groundmass. The high Mg# values (80–83) and elevated concentrations of compatible trace elements (Cr and Ni) indicate the primitive nature of the melt, whereas the incompatible trace element ratios exclude any significant crustal contamination and closely resemble those of subduction-related calc-alkaline lamprophyres reported globally. Elevated Ba/Nb and low Nb/La ratios indicate derivation from an enriched lithospheric mantle source. The pronounced negative Ti–Nb–Ta and Zr-Hf anomalies, together with diagnostic geochemical ratios (e.g., Hf/Sm, Ta/La, Nb/Yb, La/Nb, Ba/Nb), suggest that the mantle source was metasomatized predominantly by dehydrated fluids released from a subducting slab. Zircon grains from the Salem lamprophyre exhibit well-developed magmatic crystallization features, and LA–ICP–MS U-Pb analyses yield a weighted mean age of 805 ± 2.7 Ma (MSWD = 0.54), which is comparable with the Tonian (ca. 830–720 Ma) alkaline–carbonatite magmatism in the region. We envisage that the Neoarchean subduction-modified lithospheric mantle was reactivated and partially melted during Neoproterozoic extension to generate the hydrous parental magma of the Salem calc-alkaline lamprophyre.

item.title: Calcium Solubility of Bridgmanite in Subducted Basalt in Earth's Lower Mantle
item.dc:creator: Chengwei Zhang
Takashi Yoshino
Elias El Ghazaoui
Stella Chariton
Vitali B. Prakapenka
Luo Li
Yanyao Zhang
Jung‐Fu Lin
item.description: Abstract The calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the resulting abundance of davemaoite under lower‐mantle pressure‐temperature conditions have been under debate following a recent report of extensive calcium dissolution in bridgmanite. We experimentally investigated the calcium solubility in bridgmanite in a basaltic composition at pressures up to 125 GPa and temperatures up to 3,520 K. Our experimental data are modeled thermodynamically along the bridgmanite‐davemaoite solvus to quantify the calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the davemaoite proportion in basaltic and pyrolitic assemblages along lower mantle geotherms. Our results indicate that bridgmanite hosts only 0.001–0.03 Ca cations per formula unit in the lower mantle, while davemaoite remains as an abundant mineral, accounting for 8 and 25 vol% of pyrolitic and basaltic mineralogical models, respectively, along representative lower‐mantle geotherms. Our thermoelastic modeling further indicates that the effect of calcium dissolution on the density and bulk sound velocity of lower‐mantle assemblages is negligible. Published: 2026-07-10T13:21:22+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.569 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Chengwei Zhang; Takashi Yoshino; Elias El Ghazaoui; Stella Chariton; Vitali B. Prakapenka; Luo Li; Yanyao Zhang; Jung‐Fu Lin

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2026gl121981

Relevance: 0.569 (consider)

Abstract The calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the resulting abundance of davemaoite under lower‐mantle pressure‐temperature conditions have been under debate following a recent report of extensive calcium dissolution in bridgmanite. We experimentally investigated the calcium solubility in bridgmanite in a basaltic composition at pressures up to 125 GPa and temperatures up to 3,520 K. Our experimental data are modeled thermodynamically along the bridgmanite‐davemaoite solvus to quantify the calcium solubility in bridgmanite and the davemaoite proportion in basaltic and pyrolitic assemblages along lower mantle geotherms. Our results indicate that bridgmanite hosts only 0.001–0.03 Ca cations per formula unit in the lower mantle, while davemaoite remains as an abundant mineral, accounting for 8 and 25 vol% of pyrolitic and basaltic mineralogical models, respectively, along representative lower‐mantle geotherms. Our thermoelastic modeling further indicates that the effect of calcium dissolution on the density and bulk sound velocity of lower‐mantle assemblages is negligible.

item.title: Petrogenesis of ∼1.96 Ga Mafic–Intermediate Intrusions in the Daqingshan Area: Constraints on the Tectonic Evolution of the Khondalite Belt, North China Craton
item.dc:creator: Yundi Gong
Xiaojie Dong
Zhongyuan Xu
Guanghui Lian
Zhenghong Liu
Guoyu Fan
item.description: The Khondalite Belt (KB) is a Paleoproterozoic collisional orogen within the North China Craton (NCC), but its pre-collisional tectonic evolution remains poorly constrained. In this study, we conducted integrated geochronological, in-situ Hf isotopic, and whole-rock geochemical analyses of the mafic–intermediate intrusions exposed in the Daqingshan area in the central segment of the KB to determine their formation ages, petrogenesis, and tectonic setting, thereby further constraining the late Paleoproterozoic tectonic evolution of the KB. Zircon U-Pb dating results show that these rocks crystallized at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga and subsequently underwent metamorphism at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. These rocks are enriched in light rare earth elements and depleted in high field strength elements, exhibiting geochemical affinities comparable to those of continental margin arc basalts and island arc basalts. In-situ zircon Hf isotopic analyses yield positive εHf(t) values. The available data suggest that the mafic–intermediate intrusions were derived from a hybrid mantle source dominated by asthenospheric mantle and metasomatized by fluids derived from a subducted slab and experienced fractional crystallization of olivine, clinopyroxene, Fe-Ti oxides and apatite. Overall, these findings suggest that the KB was in a subduction-related tectonic setting at ∼1.97–1.96 Ga. Subsequent continent–continent collision at ∼1.95 Ga resulted in metamorphism of both the magmatic rocks and the Khondalite Series at ∼1.94–1.92 Ga. Published: 2026-07-16T15:44:11+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.566 (consider)
